guidelineName: Data Use and Sharing - Required Reason APIs
rejectionReason: Your app does not include the required PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest file.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app without the PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file, which became mandatory for all apps submitted after February 2025. The app used Required Reason APIs (UserDefaults, file timestamp access, disk space) without declaring the reasons in a privacy manifest.
whatAppleSaid: ITMS-91053: Missing API declaration - Your app's code in the binary references one or more APIs that require reasons. Starting February 2025, all apps must include a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file that declares the reasons for using Required Reason APIs. Please add the privacy manifest to your app.
fix: Created a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file and added it to the app target's Build Phases > Copy Bundle Resources. Declared all Required Reason API usages with their approved reasons (e.g., NSPrivacyAccessedAPICategoryUserDefaults with reason 'CA92.1' for app functionality). Also ensured all third-party SDKs included their own privacy manifests.
behavioralSignals: Required Reason API usage without manifest

guidelineName: Data Use and Sharing - Required Reason APIs
rejectionReason: Your PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file exists in the project but is not included in the app bundle.
whatDeveloperDid: Created the PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file and added it to the Xcode project, but forgot to add it to the target's Build Phases > Copy Bundle Resources. The file existed in the project navigator but was not included in the compiled app bundle.
whatAppleSaid: ITMS-91053: Missing API declaration - Your app references Required Reason APIs but the privacy manifest was not found in the app bundle. Please ensure the PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file is included in your app's Copy Bundle Resources build phase.
fix: Added the PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file to the correct target's Build Phases > Copy Bundle Resources. Verified it was included in the bundle by inspecting the .ipa contents. For frameworks and Swift packages, ensured each target has its own privacy manifest included in the correct location.
behavioralSignals: PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy in project but not bundle
file not in Copy Bundle Resources

guidelineName: Data Collection and Storage - Account Deletion
rejectionReason: Your app supports account creation but does not offer account deletion.
whatDeveloperDid: Built an app with user registration and login but did not provide any way for users to delete their account and associated data from within the app. Users had to email support to request account deletion.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1(v) - Legal - Privacy - Data Collection and Storage: Apps that support account creation must also offer account deletion. Your app allows users to create an account but does not provide the ability to delete the account within the app. The account deletion option must be easy to find and must initiate deletion of the account and all associated personal data.
fix: Added an 'Delete Account' option in Settings > Account. The deletion flow includes a confirmation dialog explaining what data will be deleted, a re-authentication step for security, and an optional reason selector. Account and all associated data are deleted within 14 days as per Apple's requirements. Also added the AccountDeletionURL in App Store Connect.

rejectionReason: The App Tracking Transparency prompt does not appear on iPad during review.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented ATT (App Tracking Transparency) permission request, but it only appeared on iPhone. On iPad, the ATT prompt never showed because the app was checking for IDFA availability using an older API that returned different results on iPad, or the prompt was being called before the app was in the active state on iPad.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: We noticed that your app does not display the App Tracking Transparency permission prompt on iPad. The app appears to collect tracking data without requesting user permission. Please ensure the ATT prompt appears on all supported device types.
fix: Ensured the ATT prompt is called after the app reaches the active state using applicationDidBecomeActive or sceneDidBecomeActive. Added a delay to ensure the UI is fully loaded before presenting the prompt. Tested on both iPhone and iPad to verify the prompt appears correctly on all devices.

rejectionReason: The App Tracking Transparency prompt is called at an inappropriate time and does not display.
whatDeveloperDid: Called requestTrackingAuthorization() in application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:) or during a scene transition, before the app was fully in the foreground active state. The system silently dropped the ATT prompt because the app wasn't yet active.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: The App Tracking Transparency prompt does not appear when your app is launched. The ATT prompt must be displayed while the app is in the active state. Please ensure you request tracking authorization at an appropriate time after the app has become active.
fix: Moved the ATT permission request to trigger after the app is fully active and visible. Used a slight delay (e.g., DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 1.0)) after applicationDidBecomeActive to ensure the app is ready. Showed the prompt on a meaningful screen (e.g., after onboarding) rather than immediately on launch.

rejectionReason: The App Tracking Transparency prompt is blocked by other permission dialogs.
whatDeveloperDid: Requested multiple permissions simultaneously on app launch: notifications, location, camera, and ATT. The system queued these permission dialogs, and on some devices/OS versions the ATT prompt was blocked or dismissed by other permission alerts appearing over it.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: We were unable to see the App Tracking Transparency prompt during our review. It appears that other permission requests may be interfering with the ATT prompt display. Please ensure the ATT prompt is displayed without interference from other system dialogs.
fix: Implemented a sequential permission request flow that spaces out permission prompts. Request permissions one at a time with appropriate delays between each. Show the ATT prompt on its own screen without other system dialogs. Used a permission queue manager to ensure only one system alert is presented at a time.

rejectionReason: Your app uses a third-party SDK (Adjust) that performs device fingerprinting for tracking purposes.
whatDeveloperDid: Used the Adjust SDK for attribution tracking. An older version of the SDK performed device fingerprinting (collecting device model, OS version, IP address, locale, and other signals to create a unique identifier) as a fallback when IDFA was unavailable, violating Apple's anti-tracking policies.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: Your app includes a third-party SDK (Adjust) that collects device and usage data to create a unique identifier for tracking purposes (fingerprinting). Fingerprinting is not permitted regardless of whether the user has granted tracking permission. Over 50,000 apps were affected by this enforcement action.
fix: Updated the Adjust SDK to the latest version that complies with Apple's anti-fingerprinting requirements. The updated SDK removes fingerprinting fallback mechanisms and relies solely on ATT-consented IDFA or Apple's SKAdNetwork for attribution. Verified with Adjust's compliance documentation.

rejectionReason: Your app's photo library permission description does not adequately explain why the app needs access.
whatDeveloperDid: Set the NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription to a vague message like 'App requires photo access to function' or 'This app needs your photos' without explaining specifically why the app needs photo library access and how the photos would be used.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1 - Legal - Privacy - Data Collection and Storage: Your app's permission request descriptions are not sufficiently descriptive. The purpose string for photo library access ('App requires photo access to function') does not clearly explain to users why the app needs this access. Please provide a specific, clear description of how the app uses the photo library.
fix: Updated NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription to a specific, clear description: 'YourApp needs access to your photo library to let you select photos for your profile picture and share images in chat conversations.' The description now explains exactly what the photos are used for.

rejectionReason: Your app accesses the photo library without including the required NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription.
whatDeveloperDid: The app's own code didn't access the photo library, but a third-party SDK (e.g., an image picker library, chat SDK, or analytics SDK with screenshot detection) included photo library access APIs. The developer didn't add NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription to Info.plist because they were unaware the SDK accessed photos.
whatAppleSaid: ITMS-90683: Missing Purpose String in Info.plist - Your app's code references the photo library API but the corresponding purpose string (NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription) is not present in the Info.plist. All apps that access the photo library must include a purpose string explaining the use.
fix: Added NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription to Info.plist with a clear description of how the third-party SDK uses photo access. Audited all third-party SDKs for permission requirements and ensured all required usage descriptions are present. Alternatively, if the photo access was unnecessary, configured the SDK to disable that feature or switched to an SDK that doesn't require photo access.

rejectionReason: Your VPN app uses the VPN capability primarily for content blocking or ad blocking rather than providing a VPN service.
whatDeveloperDid: Created an app that used the Network Extension VPN entitlement primarily to block ads, trackers, and specific website content by routing traffic through a local VPN tunnel with DNS-level filtering. The app was marketed as an ad blocker rather than a VPN service.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.4 - Legal - VPN Apps: Your app uses the VPN capability for purposes other than providing a virtual private network service. Using the VPN entitlement primarily for content blocking, ad blocking, or traffic manipulation is not permitted. VPN apps must provide VPN service as their primary function.
fix: Restructured the app to use Safari Content Blocker extension (Content Blocker API) for ad blocking instead of VPN-based filtering. For DNS-level filtering, used the DNSSettings configuration profile approach. Removed the VPN entitlement since the app's primary purpose was content blocking, not VPN.

rejectionReason: Your app's privacy policy URL leads to a broken page or placeholder content.
whatDeveloperDid: Provided a privacy policy URL in App Store Connect that returned a 404 error, was a placeholder page ('Coming Soon'), or redirected to the app's homepage without any privacy policy content.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1 - Legal - Privacy - Data Collection and Storage: The privacy policy URL provided for your app does not lead to a valid privacy policy. The URL returns a 404 error. All apps must provide a working privacy policy URL that is accessible to users.
fix: Created a proper privacy policy page and hosted it at a stable, permanent URL. Ensured the URL is accessible, loads quickly, and contains a complete privacy policy. Updated the privacy policy URL in App Store Connect and within the app settings.
behavioralSignals: broken link in App Store Connect

rejectionReason: Your privacy policy does not adequately describe the data your app collects and how it is used.
whatDeveloperDid: Provided a generic one-paragraph privacy policy that did not specify what data the app collects, how it's used, whether it's shared with third parties, data retention periods, or user rights regarding their data.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1 - Legal - Privacy - Data Collection and Storage: Your privacy policy does not adequately describe your app's data collection and usage practices. The privacy policy must clearly identify what data is collected, how it is used, when it is shared, and the data retention policy. Please update your privacy policy to include these details.
fix: Rewrote the privacy policy to include: specific types of data collected (personal info, usage data, device info), purposes of data collection, third-party services and SDKs that receive data, data retention and deletion policies, user rights (access, correction, deletion), contact information for privacy inquiries, and effective date.

rejectionReason: Your app accesses location data in the background for analytics purposes rather than a user-facing feature.
whatDeveloperDid: Requested 'Always' location permission and tracked the user's location in the background to feed location data into an analytics platform for market research, without providing any user-facing feature that required background location.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: Your app collects location data in the background for analytics and advertising purposes rather than providing a user-facing feature. Background location access must be tied to a feature that directly benefits the user. Using location for analytics or advertising is not a valid use of background location.
fix: Removed background location tracking entirely. Downgraded to 'While Using' location permission. Location data is now only used for the app's core map feature while the user is actively using the app. Removed location data from the analytics pipeline.

rejectionReason: Your app reads clipboard contents without clear user intent or action.
whatDeveloperDid: The app read the clipboard contents (UIPasteboard.general.string) on every app launch or foreground event to detect URLs, referral codes, or other content. Starting with iOS 16, users see a paste notification banner and starting with iOS 14, a paste indicator appears, making this behavior highly visible.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1 - Legal - Privacy - Data Collection and Storage: Your app reads the clipboard contents on launch without a clear user action or intent. Reading clipboard data should only occur in response to an explicit user action (e.g., tapping a paste button). Automatically reading the clipboard on app launch is not permitted.
fix: Removed automatic clipboard reading on launch. Implemented a 'Paste' button that users can tap to voluntarily share clipboard contents. Used the new UIPasteboard.DetectionPattern API (iOS 14+) to detect if the clipboard contains relevant content (like URLs) without actually reading it, then show a prompt for the user to paste.

rejectionReason: Your app shares user data with third-party SDKs that are not disclosed in your privacy nutrition labels.
whatDeveloperDid: Integrated multiple third-party SDKs (analytics, advertising, crash reporting) that collected and shared user data, but did not disclose this data sharing in the App Privacy (nutrition label) section of App Store Connect. The privacy labels showed 'Data Not Collected' while SDKs were actively collecting and transmitting user data.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: Your app's privacy information on the App Store does not accurately reflect your app's data collection and sharing practices. We identified third-party SDKs in your app that collect data not disclosed in your privacy nutrition labels. Please update your App Privacy information to accurately reflect all data collection.
fix: Audited all third-party SDKs for data collection practices using each SDK's privacy documentation. Updated the App Privacy nutrition labels in App Store Connect to accurately reflect all data collected by the app and its SDKs, including the purpose of collection and whether it's linked to the user's identity.
behavioralSignals: privacy labels don't match SDK data collection
Data Not Collected but SDKs present
